patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch limits the iterations of the `shrink_delalloc` function in Btrfs to prevent it from blocking indefinitely, especially when dealing with large amounts of writeback data. The function is modified to process delalloc work in units of 128MiB, rather than trying to reclaim large chunks at once. This change aims to strike a balance between making progress on space reclaim and avoiding arbitrarily long syscall latencies and task hangs.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] btrfs: make inode->outstanding_extents a u64

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the data type of 'outstanding_extents' in the Btrfs inode structure from u32 to u64. This change is motivated by the fact that the maximum file size is LLONG_MAX, which results in a computation bound above by ~2^63 / 2^27. With recent changes to divide by 128KiB in compressed cases, this bound is even higher. The patch also updates the 'mod_outstanding_extents' function to handle u64 inputs and adds assertions to ensure that the inputs are valid. This change aims to capture the intent of the types and make the code more robust.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] btrfs: account for compression in delalloc extent reservation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue in the Btrfs file system where delayed reference reservations were under-estimated due to compression. Specifically, when COMPRESS_FORCE is set or the inode is configured to always compress, the maximum compressed extent size (128KiB) is significantly smaller than the maximum uncompressed extent size (128MiB). The patch introduces a new function `btrfs_inode_max_extents` that takes into account the compression type and returns the correct number of extents for reservation. This change prevents under-reservation of delayed refs reservations for delalloc writes, even when compression is forced. Additionally, the patch removes an unnecessary shim function `count_max_extents` and simplifies the code in the process.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] btrfs: reserve space for delayed_refs in delalloc

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue in the Btrfs file system where space is not reserved for delayed references (delayed_refs) during delalloc operations. Delalloc uses a per-inode block reservation to anticipate metadata needs, but it does not account for delayed refs. As a result, when writeback occurs and delayed refs are generated, they draw from an under-reserved trans_handle's delayed refs reservation. This can lead to incorrect assessments of reserved space in the metadata space reclaim logic, causing poor decision making and blocking file system operations.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] btrfs: improve stalls under sudden writeback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve stalls under sudden writeback in Btrfs file systems by addressing issues related to delalloc work and metadata space system. The patches introduce targeted improvements, including better reservations for delayed refs and smoothing of the work in flush_space(). This is achieved through four patches: (1) reserving space for delayed refs in delalloc, (2) accounting for compression in delalloc extent reservation, (3) making inode->outstanding_extents a u64, and (4) capping shrink_delalloc iterations. The changes are designed to reduce stalls on systems with large memory and disk sizes, where sudden writeback can cause system-wide issues.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

review_comments.author: Anand Jain
review_comments.summary: Anand points out that BLK_STS_PROTECTION is interpreted differently depending on the device type and suggests that the patch is NVMe-specific.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 7/4/26 00:15, Boris Burkov wrote:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 

> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().


BLK_STS_PROTECTION is interpreted differently depending on the device.
In NVMe, it indicates invalid Protection Information (PI), while in
HDDs/SSDs it typically points to a malformed CDB (often a software bug).
This is one of several possible error conditions.

Rather than introducing new BLK_STS_* codes, vendors have reused
existing ones for newer device types to maintain backward compatibility.

There isn't much we can do about this for now. I assume the malformed
CDB-related issues have largely been resolved, so this Btrfs fix is
effectively NVMe-specific.

Reviewed-by: Anand Jain <asj@kernel.org>

Thanks
Anand

> which we expect won't really happen.
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 5/5] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to spinning locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 5/5] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to spinning locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to queued spinlocks and rwlocks, allowing for tracing of lock contention events. The patch uses an arch-overridable function to ensure correct behavior across all architectures, including those with custom unlock implementations. When the tracepoint is disabled, it has minimal impact on performance, adding only a single NOP instruction to the hot path. However, when enabled, it can increase binary size by up to 0.24% due to the need to preserve the lock pointer in a callee-saved register across the trace call.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 4/5] locking: Factor out queued_spin_release()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 4/5] locking: Factor out queued_spin_release()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function called queued_spin_release(), which is an arch-overridable unlock primitive. It renames existing arch-specific queued_spin_unlock() overrides on x86 (paravirt) and MIPS to queued_spin_release(). The change is preparatory for the next commit, which will add contended release tracepoint instrumentation to queued_spin_unlock(). No functional changes are made.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 3/5] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 3/5] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking subsystem. The contended_release tracepoint fires on the holder side when a contended lock is released, allowing for correlation of lock hold time under contention with waiter events by lock address. The patch modifies the slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks (mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks) to fire the tracepoint before releasing the lock or waking the waiter. This enables more detailed tracing and debugging of locking behavior in the kernel.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 0/5] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 0/5] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides two main benefits: it allows for hold time estimation by correlating contention_end and contended_release events, and it captures the holder's stack at release time, which can be different from what is captured earlier if the holder does significant work between the waiter's arrival and unlock. The patch also fixes several issues in the previous versions, including spurious events, use-after-free errors, and build failures on certain architectures.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Matthew Wilcox questioned the usefulness of the contended_release tracepoint, specifically asking for explanations on how it provides value in measuring hold time and capturing the holder's stack at release time.
review_comments.raw_body:
As someone who's not an expert in this area (so please use short words
to explain it to me), why do we want to know how long this holder took
to release the lock from when it became contended?

I understand why we want to know how long any given waiter had to wait
to gain the lock (but we already have tracepoints which show that).

I also don't understand why we want to know the holder's stack at
release time.  The stack at contention-begin time will include
the point at which the lock was acquired which should be correlated
with where the lock was released.

Perhaps examples might help me understand why we want this?

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
Steven Rostedt noted that the tracepoint placement for queued spinlocks and rwlocks should fire before the lock is released, not after, to accurately capture the holder's stack at release time.

The reviewer, Steven Rostedt, pointed out that the contended_release tracepoint would be useful for determining how long a critical section is held when a lock is highly contended by many tasks. He suggested that seeing the release time and location would help identify if the length of the critical section is causing the contention.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 26 Mar 2026 15:55:21 +0000
Matthew Wilcox <willy@infradead.org> wrote:

---

Dmitry could give his own rationale for this, but I have my only use case.

This would be useful to find out how long the critical section is. If a
lock is highly contended by many tasks, you could get a high contention
time simply because other tasks are causing the delay for the waiter.
Seeing the release time and location would let you also know how long the
critical section was held, and if the length of the critical section is
causing the contention.

Having a stack trace of the release would differentiate the path that
released the lock, as there can be many places that release them. Although,
I have to admit, I'm not sure there are many different places locks are
released. Especially now that we have guard(), which will make all the
releases in a function at the same location.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author is addressing Matthew Wilcox's suggestion to come up with generic instrumentation for contended release tracepoint, explaining that the current implementation provides a cause (holder time) and symptom (waiter time), but requires different treatments depending on the lock usage pattern.

The author addressed Matthew Wilcox's concern that the --lock-owner feature does not work for all lock types by explaining that contended_release tracepoint fires in the holder's context, providing the holder's stack at release time, which is necessary to identify the holders and what they were doing.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the simplest way to think about it is the following. Waiter time
is the symptom, while holder time is the cause.

The waiter-side contention_begin/contention_end tells us how long a
waiter waited, but that time can span multiple holders.

If a waiter waited 10 ms, we can not tell whether one holder held the
lock for 10 ms or five holders held it for 2 ms each. These need
different treatments: the first means shrink the critical section, the
second means reduce lock frequency or split the lock. Today we can not
distinguish between these cases from waiter-side data alone.

---

Holder's stack allows us to understand who exactly waiters were waiting
for to release the lock.

The stack at contention_begin time does not always include the holder's
stack. The --lock-owner feature works by reading the owner field from
the lock struct, but it only supports mutex and rwsem. For spinlocks,
queued rwlocks, semaphores, and several others, the waiter has no
visibility into the holder whatsoever.

contended_release fires in the holder's context, so we get the holder's
stack at release time. For spinlocks, this is the only way to get any
holder-side information.

Original motivation was zone lock contention (a spinlock) in Meta
production workloads. We could see waiters were blocked, but had no way
to identify the holders or what they were doing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 00/24] vfs/nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations, allowing clients to register interest in a directory and receive notifications about changes within it without losing their lease. The patches introduce new functionality to the Linux kernel's NFS server (nfsd) to handle directory events such as creates, unlinks, and renames, and to send updated directory attributes in notifications. This is built on top of previous work on directory delegation and has been tested with pynfs. The series includes 24 patches that modify various parts of the kernel, including file locking, notification handling, and attribute encoding.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the server recalling delegations for directory change events by adding support to ignore lease breaks on such events. They modified try_break_deleg() and __break_lease() to take an additional flags parameter, which allows ignoring certain delegation break events when a client requests a directory delegation with notification bits covering directory changes.

review_comments.raw_body:
...mostly to show the LEASE_BREAK_* flags.

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 fs/locks.c                      |  2 ++
 include/trace/events/filelock.h | 33 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 2 files changed, 35 insertions(+)

diff --git a/fs/locks.c b/fs/locks.c
index dafa0752fdce..5af6dca2d46c 100644
--- a/fs/locks.c
+++ b/fs/locks.c
@@ -1654,6 +1654,8 @@ int __break_lease(struct inode *inode, unsigned int flags)
 	bool want_write = !(flags & LEASE_BREAK_OPEN_RDONLY);
 	int error = 0;
 
+	trace_break_lease(inode, flags);
+
 	if (flags & LEASE_BREAK_LEASE)
 		type = FL_LEASE;
 	else if (flags & LEASE_BREAK_DELEG)
diff --git a/include/trace/events/filelock.h b/include/trace/events/filelock.h
index ef4bb0afb86a..fff0ee2d452d 100644
--- a/include/trace/events/filelock.h
+++ b/include/trace/events/filelock.h
@@ -120,6 +120,39 @@ DEFINE_EVENT(filelock_lock, flock_lock_inode,
 		TP_PROTO(struct inode *inode, struct file_lock *fl, int ret),
 		TP_ARGS(inode, fl, ret));
 
+#define show_lease_break_flags(val)					\
+	__print_flags(val, "|",						\
+		{ LEASE_BREAK_LEASE,		"LEASE" },		\
+		{ LEASE_BREAK_DELEG,		"DELEG" },		\
+		{ LEASE_BREAK_LAYOUT,		"LAYOUT" },		\
+		{ LEASE_BREAK_NONBLOCK,		"NONBLOCK" },		\
+		{ LEASE_BREAK_OPEN_RDONLY,	"OPEN_RDONLY" },	\
+		{ LEASE_BREAK_DIR_CREATE,	"DIR_CREATE" },		\
+		{ LEASE_BREAK_DIR_DELETE,	"DIR_DELETE" },		\
+		{ LEASE_BREAK_DIR_RENAME,	"DIR_RENAME" })
+
+TRACE_EVENT(break_lease,
+	TP_PROTO(struct inode *inode, unsigned int flags),
+
+	TP_ARGS(inode, flags),
+
+	TP_STRUCT__entry(
+		__field(unsigned long, i_ino)
+		__field(dev_t, s_dev)
+		__field(unsigned int, flags)
+	),
+
+	TP_fast_assign(
+		__entry->s_dev = inode->i_sb->s_dev;
+		__entry->i_ino = inode->i_ino;
+		__entry->flags = flags;
+	),
+
+	TP_printk("dev=0x%x:0x%x ino=0x%lx flags=%s",
+		  MAJOR(__entry->s_dev), MINOR(__entry->s_dev),
+		  __entry->i_ino, show_lease_break_flags(__entry->flags))
+);
+
 DECLARE_EVENT_CLASS(filelock_lease,
 	TP_PROTO(struct inode *inode, struct file_lease *fl),
 

--

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about enabling the correct notification types when requesting a directory lease, and provided a patch to modify nfs4_alloc_init_lease() to use a new function nfsd_notify_to_ignore() that sets the FL_IGN_DIR_* flags based on the requested notification types. The author agreed to make this change.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about modifying an existing mark's mask when setting or unsetting directory delegations, and added a new exported function to handle this modification. The function takes in the mark, bits to set, and bits to clear, and updates the mask accordingly. If changes were made, it also recalculates the mark's associated connector.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about updating the fsnotify mark when directory delegations are added or removed, by adding a new helper function nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask() that updates the mask on the nfsd_file's fsnotify_mark to be a union of all current directory delegations on an inode. The function will be called when directory delegations are added or removed.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about handling allocation failures in CB_NOTIFY operations by changing the ->prepare callback operation to return true if processing should continue and false otherwise, which will be implemented in the patch.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch introduces a potential deadlock between nfsd and the client, as the server sends CB_NOTIFY events to the client while holding the dir delegation lock, which can cause a lock ordering violation if the client is trying to reclaim memory

Gave Reviewed-by
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, 07 Apr 2026 09:21:27 -0400
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org> wrote:

series_items.subject: [PATCH 01/24] filelock: add support for ignoring deleg breaks for dir change events
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support to ignore delegation breaks for directory change events in the Linux kernel's filelock subsystem. It introduces a new flags parameter to try_break_deleg() and modifies __break_lease to ignore certain types of delegation break events, such as directory creation, deletion, or renaming. This allows NFS clients to be notified of directory changes after the fact instead of having their delegations recalled immediately. The patch also includes several code modifications to implement this new behavior.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 02/24] filelock: add a tracepoint to start of break_lease()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a tracepoint to the start of the break_lease() function in the Linux kernel's filelock module. The tracepoint is used to display information about the LEASE_BREAK_* flags that are being passed to the function. This allows for easier debugging and analysis of lease breaking operations, which can be complex and involve multiple flags. The patch adds a new trace event called break_lease, which includes fields for the inode's device number, inode number, and the flags being used. The patch also defines a helper macro show_lease_break_flags to format the flags in a human-readable way.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/24] filelock: add an inode_lease_ignore_mask helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function called `inode_lease_ignore_mask` to the Linux kernel's filelock module. The function returns a mask of all directory change events that are currently ignored by any leases on a given inode. This is useful for NFSd, which will use this information to configure the fsnotify_mark mask. The patch introduces a new routine that walks through the list of leases associated with an inode and ors together their ignored event flags. The function is exported as a GPL symbol.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 04/24] nfsd: add protocol support for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds protocol support for CB_NOTIFY in the nfsd module. It updates the xdr files and removes duplicate definitions from various header files. The changes include adding new enum values, updating typedefs, and regenerating the xdr files. This is part of a larger series of patches that aim to improve the NFSv4.x protocol implementation in the Linux kernel.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 05/24] nfs_common: add new NOTIFY4_* flags proposed in RFC8881bis
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new flags to the NFSv4 protocol, specifically NOTIFY4_* flags proposed in RFC8881bis. These flags are added to the GET_DIR_DELEGATION operation and include various notification types such as AUFLAG_VALID, AUFLAG_USER, and CHANGE_AUTH. The changes also update the XDR specification file and the generated code for the nfsd kernel module. This patch is part of a larger series that aims to support new features in NFSv4.1 bis document.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/24] nfsd: allow nfsd to get a dir lease with an ignore mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the NFS daemon to obtain a directory lease with an ignore mask, enabling it to filter out specific notification types such as file creation, deletion, and renaming. The ignore mask is determined by the requested notification types, allowing for more fine-grained control over which events trigger notifications. This change affects the nfsd component of the Linux kernel.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/24] vfs: add fsnotify_modify_mark_mask()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called fsnotify_modify_mark_mask() to the Linux kernel's vfs (Virtual File System) module. The function allows callers to modify an existing mark's mask by setting and clearing specific bits, and it also updates the associated connector if necessary. This is done to support NFSd's need to modify directory delegation settings on existing marks. The patch includes a new exported function in fs/notify/mark.c and adds a reference to this function in include/linux/fsnotify_backend.h.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/24] nfsd: update the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a dir delegation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the fsnotify mark on an nfsd_file object when setting or removing a directory delegation. It adds a new helper function, nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask(), that recalculates the mask based on the current delegations and ignores events as specified by the inode's lease mask. The function is called in two places: when unlocking a delegation lease and when getting a directory delegation. This ensures that fsnotify notifications are updated correctly to reflect changes in directory delegations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/24] nfsd: make nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation bool return
series_items.patch_summary: This patch changes the nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation to return a boolean value indicating whether processing should continue or not. The patch modifies several callback operations, including CB_NOTIFY, layout preparation, and recall preparation, to return true if successful and false otherwise. This change allows for better error handling in case of allocation failures during callback processing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/24] nfsd: add callback encoding and decoding linkages for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds routines for encoding and decoding CB_NOTIFY messages in the NFSv4 callback protocol. It introduces new functions to encode and decode the CB_NOTIFY message, which is used to notify a client of changes on the server. The encoding and decoding processes involve calling into code generated by xdrgen to perform the actual encoding and decoding. This patch also updates the nfsd/nfs4callback.c file to include the new encoding and decoding functions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 11/24] nfsd: use RCU to protect fi_deleg_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds RCU (Read-Copy Update) protection to the fi_deleg_file pointer in the nfsd kernel module. The patch annotates the pointer with __rcu and converts all accessors to use proper RCU primitives, such as rcu_assign_pointer() and rcu_dereference_protected(). This change is necessary because concurrent accesses from workqueue callbacks can dereference a NULL pointer if they race with delegation teardown. The patch prepares for a subsequent change that will safely acquire a reference from the CB_NOTIFY callback path without holding the fi_lock lock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 12/24] nfsd: add data structures for handling CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds data structures and functions to support directory delegation CB_NOTIFY in the NFS server. It introduces two new structures: `nfsd_notify_event` for carrying fsnotify events and `nfsd4_cb_notify` for per-delegation state handling. The patch also refactors the `alloc_init_deleg` function into a common base with a pluggable callback, adds allocation helpers for directory delegations, and sets up skeleton operations for notification handling.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 13/24] nfsd: add notification handlers for dir events
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds notification handlers for directory events in the NFS daemon (nfsd). It introduces a new fsnotify group for directories and modifies existing code to handle directory creation, deletion, and modification notifications. The patch also allocates a nfsd_notify_event object and sets up callback functions to handle these notifications. If any errors occur during this process, it recalls affected delegations. This change aims to improve the handling of directory events in NFS, enabling more robust and efficient notification mechanisms.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 14/24] nfsd: add tracepoint to dir_event handler
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint to the NFS daemon's dir_event handler, providing extra visibility into file system notifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 15/24] nfsd: apply the notify mask to the delegation when requested
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the NFSv4 server to apply the requested notification mask to the delegation when a client requests a directory delegation with notifications enabled. The patch ensures that if the client doesn't set the NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND flag, no notifications are offered, as the server won't provide necessary information for classic notifications. The patch also defines a constant for supported notify masks and updates the get_dir_delegation function to apply the requested notification mask.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 16/24] nfsd: add helper to marshal a fattr4 from completed args
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code that encodes attribute values in an NFSv4 file attribute (fattr4) from a completed operation. It breaks out the encoding of actual attr_vals into a separate function, nfsd4_encode_attr_vals(), which takes a struct xdr_stream and a u32 bitmap as arguments. This change simplifies the code and makes it easier to maintain.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 17/24] nfsd: allow nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change() to work with no export
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change function to work even when there is no export available, which can happen in certain callback scenarios such as CB_NOTIFY. This is safe because nfsd will not grant a delegation on the V4 root. The change involves modifying the condition under which an error is returned from this function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 18/24] nfsd: send basic file attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to send basic inode information in a CB_NOTIFY event, including attributes that can be acquired via GETATTR. The patch introduces new code to handle this functionality and includes error handling for cases where the client was granted a delegation when a file was created. The changes are made to the nfsd4xdr.c file in the Linux kernel.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 19/24] nfsd: allow encoding a filehandle into fattr4 without a svc_fh
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the encoding of a filehandle into fattr4 without requiring a svc_fh. It achieves this by adding a new 'fhandle' field to struct nfsd4_fattr_args and copying the filehandle into it from the svc_fh in CB_NOTIFY callbacks. This change enables the encoding of filehandles in scenarios where a svc_fh is not available, such as in CB_NOTIFY callbacks.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 20/24] nfsd: add a fi_connectable flag to struct nfs4_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag, fi_connectable, to the nfs4_file structure in the Linux NFS server. The flag indicates whether a filehandle is connectable or not, which is necessary for encoding filehandles in certain situations, such as CB_NOTIFY operations. The patch modifies the nfsd4_file_init function to set this flag based on the export flags of the underlying directory delegation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 21/24] nfsd: add the filehandle to returned attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new routine to compose a filehandle in the context of a CB_NOTIFY, where there is no export to consult. The routine uses only the parent filehandle and nfs4_file to fill out the fhandle field in the nfsd4_fattr_args. This allows for the return of a filehandle in the attributes of a CB_NOTIFY response.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 22/24] nfsd: properly track requested child attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of child attributes in NFSv4 delegation by tracking the union of requested and supported attributes, and only encoding the attributes in that union when sending add/remove/rename updates. This ensures that only relevant attributes are sent to the client, reducing unnecessary data transfer and improving performance. The patch also introduces new macros to define the bitmasks for child attributes, making the code more readable and maintainable.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 23/24] nfsd: track requested dir attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of directory attributes in NFSv4 by tracking the union of requested and supported dir attributes in the delegation, and only encoding the attributes in that union when sending add/remove/rename updates. This ensures that only valid and supported attributes are sent to the client, preventing potential errors or inconsistencies. The patch also introduces new data structures to store the directory attributes and notification masks, making it easier to manage and update these values. Overall, this patch enhances the reliability and robustness of NFSv4 by improving its handling of directory attributes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes in the NFSv4 server. It allows clients that have requested notifications of directory attribute changes to receive these updates alongside other events such as add, remove, and rename operations. The server still recalls the delegation on a SETATTR operation, so these notifications are only sent when there is a change to child directories. The patch modifies two files: nfsd/nfs4state.c and fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c, adding new code to handle directory attribute changes and update the notify mask accordingly.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] dcache: warn when a dentry is freed with a non-empty ->d_lru

version_history.subject: [PATCH] dcache: warn when a dentry is freed with a non-empty ->d_lru

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a WARN_ON_ONCE() in the dentry_free() function to warn when a dentry is freed with a non-empty ->d_lru list. The warning is intended to detect and prevent use-after-free (UAF) bugs, which have been observed in certain scenarios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] nfs: fix utimensat() for atime with delegated timestamps
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS client where utimensat() operations that only update the atime attribute do not properly update the ctime when delegated timestamps are enabled. The problem arises because there is no mechanism to update the ctime alongside atime-only changes. To fix this, the patch modifies the client to issue an RPC to update the ctime in such cases. This ensures that the ctime is correctly updated along with the atime.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] nfs: update inode ctime after removexattr operation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the inode's ctime (last modification time) after a removexattr operation on an NFS file system. The problem it solves is that the stat() function does not trigger a GETATTR request when delegated timestamps are used, resulting in an incorrect cached ctime value. To fix this, the patch adds a trailing GETATTR to the removexattr compound, similar to how setxattr works. This ensures that the inode's ctime is updated correctly after removing an extended attribute.

She suggested that the patch relies on receiving an updated value from the server.

review_comments.raw_body:

not update its ctime/mtime for WRITE, CLONE, COPY. Is possible that
some implementations might be different and also do not update the

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that delegated timestamps may not cover SETATTR style operations and asks for clarification on server requirements, specifically regarding disabling timestamp updates for such operations.
review_comments.raw_body:
(cc'ing Tom)

That is a very good point.

My interpretation was that delegated timestamps generally covered
writes, but SETATTR style operations that do anything beyond only
changing the mtime can't be cached.

We probably need some delstid spec clarification: for what operations
is the server required to disable timestamp updates when a write
delegation is outstanding?

In the case of nfsd, we disable timestamp updates for WRITE/COPY/CLONE
but not SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR.

How does the Hammerspace anvil behave? Does it disable c/mtime updates
for writes when there is an outstanding timestamp delegation like we're
doing in nfsd? If so, does it do the same for
SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR operations as well?

review_comments.author: Thomas Haynes
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Thomas Haynes noted that the patch's approach to updating inode ctime after removexattr operation does not align with the delegated timestamp model described in RFC8881, where the server should query the client via CB_GETATTR to return updated timestamps while the delegation is outstanding. He suggested re-evaluating the semantics for metadata-style ops like CLONE/COPY/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR and considering whether delegated timestamps follow the same attribute-authority model as delegated size.
review_comments.raw_body:
Jeff,

I think the right way to look at this is closer to how size is
handled under delegation in RFC8881, rather than as a per-op rule.

In our implementation, because we are acting as an MDS and data I/O
goes to DSes, we already treat size as effectively delegated when
a write layout is outstanding. The MDS does not maintain authoritative
size locally in that case. We may refresh size/timestamps internally
(e.g., on GETATTR by querying DSes), but we don\u2019t treat that as
overriding the delegated authority.

For timestamps, our behavior is effectively the same model. When
the client holds the relevant delegation, the server does not
consider itself authoritative for ctime/mtime. If current values
are needed, we can obtain them from the client (e.g., via CB_GETATTR),
and the client must present the delegation stateid to demonstrate
that authority. So the authority follows the delegation, not the
specific operation.

That said, I don\u2019t think we\u2019ve fully resolved the semantics for all
metadata-style ops either. WRITE and SETATTR are clear in our model,
but for things like CLONE/COPY/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR, we\u2019ve likely
been relying on assumptions rather than a fully consistent rule.
I.e., CLONE and COPY we just pass through to the DS and we don't
implement SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR.

So the spec question, as I see it, is not whether REMOVEXATTR (or
any particular op) should update ctime/mtime, but whether delegated
timestamps are meant to follow the same attribute-authority model
as delegated size in RFC8881. If so, then we expect that the server
should query the client via CB_GETATTR to return updated ctime/mtime
after such operations while the delegation is outstanding.

Thanks,
Tom

review_comments.author: Olga Kornievskaia
review_comments.summary: Olga Kornievskaia questioned whether the server should query the client for attribute updates while the client holds an attribute delegation, specifically in cases where a CLONE operation is performed with a GETATTR attached.
review_comments.raw_body:
What happens when the client holding the attribute delegation (it's
the authority) is doing the query? Is it the server's responsibility
to query the client before replying. Example, client sends a CLONE
operation which has a GETATTR attached. (1) is the server supposed to
issue a CB_GETATTR before replying to the compound? (2) is the client
not supposed to send any GETATTRs while holding an attribute
delegation? CLONE is a modifying operation but client hasn't done any
actual modifications to the opened file so a CB_GETATTR would return
that file hasn't been modified. Is the server then not going to
express that the file has indeed been modified when replying to
GETATTR?

review_comments.author: Thomas Haynes
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that Hammerspace server may send CB_GETATTR before responding to getattr, and suggested implementing a shortcut for SETATTR in the same compound as a potential optimization

Reviewer Thomas Haynes noted that the server needs to query the values of the attributes before sending them, as it cannot assume the client wants to know what the server thinks.
review_comments.raw_body:
The Hammerspace server on a getattr checks to see if there is
a delegated stateid (and whether it is the client making the request
or not). If it is and the GETATTR asks for FATTR4_SIZE, FATTR4_TIME_MODIFY,
or FATTR4_TIME_METADATA, then it will send a CB_GETATTR before
responding to the client.

While it does not do so, if the client sent in a SETATTR in the
same compound we could short circuit that. Think a sort of WCC.

---

The server could argue that the client wants to know what it thinks.
But that isn't the argeement. The server has to query those values
before sending them on.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about timestamp delegation on SETXATTR and REMOVEXATTR operations, explaining that these need to be done synchronously due to potential failures and lack of error handling mechanisms. They proposed ignoring the timestamp delegation on the server side and updating the ctime manually, with the client sending a trailing GETATTR to retrieve the updated change attribute.
review_comments.raw_body:
The dilemma we have is: because we _do_ allow local processes to stat()
files that have an outstanding write delegation, we can never allow the
ctime in particular to roll backward (modulo clock jumps).

If we're dealing with changes that have been cached in the client and
are being lazily flushed out, then we can't update the timestamp when
that operation occurs. The time of the RPC to flush the changes will
almost certainly be later than the cached timestamps on the client that
will eventually be set, so when the client comes back we'd end up
violating the rollback rule.

Our only option is to freeze timestamp updates on anything that might
represent such an operation. So far, we only do that on WRITE and COPY
operations -- in general, operations that require an open file, since
FMODE_NOCMTIME is attached to the file.

Some SETATTRs that only update the mtime and atime can be cached on the
client by virtue of the fact that it's authoritative for timestamps.
There are some exceptions though:

- atime-only updates can't be cached since the ctime won't change with
a timestamp update if the mtime didn't change

- if you set the mtime to a time that is later than the time you got
the delegation from the server, but earlier than the current time, you
can't cache that. The ctime would be later than the mtime in that case,
and we don't have a mechanism to handle that in a delegated timestamp
SETATTR.

I don't see how you could reasonably buffer a SETXATTR or REMOVEXATTR
operation to be sent later. These need to be done synchronously since
they could always fail for some reason and we don't have a mechanism at
the syscall layer to handle a deferred error. They also only update the
ctime and not the mtime, and we have no mechanism to do that with
delegated timestamps.

Based on that, I think the client and server both need to ignore the
timestamp delegation on a SETXATTR or REMOVEXATTR. The server should
update the ctime and the client needs to send a trailing GETATTR on the
REMOVEXATTR compound in order to get it and the change attr.

Exactly what this patch does, fwiw...
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Olga Kornievskaia
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Olga Kornievskaia questioned the necessity of sending CB_GETATTR from the server to the client when the client is making a GETATTR request with a delegated stateid, citing potential counter-intuitive behavior and additional traffic. She also suggested that the client may be at fault for sending a GETATTR in the CLONE compound.
review_comments.raw_body:
So... while I might not be running the latest Hammerspace bits and
something has changed but I do not see Hammerspace server sending a
CB_GETATTR when the "client is making a getattr request with a
delegated stateid". Example is the CLONE operation with an accompanied
GETATTR. The server in this case updates the change attribute and
mtime and returns different from what the client had previously in the
OPEN back to the client (this is what the test expect but I think
that's contradictory to what is said above). (To contrast nfsd server
does not update the change attribute or mtime and returns the same
value and thus making xfstest fail). So if the spec mandates that
before answering the GETATTR a CB_GETATTR needs to be sent then
neither of the server (Hammerspace nor linux) do that. But then again
I'm not sure the client is not at fault for sending a GETATTR in the
CLONE compound in the first place. For instance client does not have a
GETATTR in the COPY compound (and then fails to pass xfstest that
checks for modifies ctime/mtime). But the solution isn't clear to is
it like Jeff's REMOVEXATTR approach to add the GETATTR to the COPY
compound but that then should trigger a CB_GETATTR back to the client.
Again probably none of the servers do that...

Another point I would like to raise is: doesn't it seem
counter-intuitive to be in a situation where we have a
delegation-holding client sending a GETATTR and then a server needing
to do a CB_GETTATTR to the said client to fulfill the request.
Delegations were supposed to reduce traffic and now we are introducing
additional traffic instead. I guess I'm arguing that the client is
broken to ever query for change_attr, mtime if it's holding the
delegation. Yet the linux client (I could be wrong here) is written
such that change_attr or mtime has to always come from the server. Say
the application did a write() followed by a stat(). Client can't
satisfy stat() without reaching out to the server. Yet the server is
not the authority and before it can generate the reply for
change_attr, mtime, it needs to callback to the client (CB_GETATTR).
It seems it would have been better to never get a delegated attribute
delegation in the first place?

review_comments.author: Thomas Haynes
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Thomas Haynes noted that delegated timestamps may not guarantee visible ctime rollback due to potential differences in authoritative timestamp generation and object changes during CB_GETATTR window, suggesting either monotonic clamping of exposed ctime or treating change_attr as the serialization signal.

Reviewer Thomas Haynes expressed concern that defining delegated timestamp behavior on a per-operation basis may limit extensibility, suggesting instead to define the behavior based on operation properties such as synchronous visibility and caching.
review_comments.raw_body:
I agree we do not want visible ctime rollback, but I do not see how
that can be guaranteed from delegated timestamps alone when the
authoritative timestamp may be generated on a different node with
a different clock and the object may change during the CB_GETATTR
window. That seems to require either monotonic clamping of exposed
ctime, or treating change_attr rather than ctime as the real
serialization signal.

---

One concern I have with a per-op formulation is extensibility. If
delegated timestamp behavior is defined by enumerating specific
operations, then every new operation added to the protocol creates
a fresh ambiguity until the spec is updated again. It seems better
to define the behavior in terms of operation properties - e.g., whether
the operation is synchronously visible, can be deferred/cached at
the client, and whether it affects only ctime versus mtime/atime -
so future operations can be classified without reopening the base
rule.

I.e., I can't tell if you want me to update the spec with
guidance per-op or you are just documenting what you did.

review_comments.author: Thomas Haynes
review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned why the patch asks the server for the inode's ctime after removexattr operation when the client could instead change its behavior to request the updated ctime, and suggested that the client should be the authority on this matter
review_comments.raw_body:
Or the client needs to change its behaviour in this scenario? Why ask
as question to a source which is not the authority?

Or, I agree with your analysis...

review_comments.author: Olga Kornievskaia
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch fails to update inode ctime because it cannot generate the necessary values, specifically the change_attr value and mtime, which should be generated by the server
review_comments.raw_body:
well i think it's because it's not able to generate the values needed
(ie the server is the only one that can generate the change_attr value
and mtime is also supposed to come from the server clock too)?

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the need for synchronous removexattr operations due to lack of delayed error reporting and potential syscall failures, but is unsure how to phrase this guidance in the spec.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think we probably need some guidance in the spec, and I think that
guidance comes down to: operations that don't have a way to report a
delayed error condition can't be buffered on the client and must
continue to be done synchronously even if a delegation is held.

By way of example: if I do a write() on the client I can buffer that
because userland can eventually do an fsync() to see if it succeeded.
This is not true for syscalls like setxattr() or removexattr(), or most
syscalls that result in a SETATTR operation (chmod(), chown(), etc).

They must be done synchronously because:

1/ there's no way to update only the ctime in a delegated timestamp
update

...and...

2/ these syscalls can fail, so we can't return from them until we know
the outcome.

How best to phrase this guidance, I'm not sure...
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Thomas Haynes
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Thomas Haynes noted that the patch's approach to distinguishing between operations that can be deferred and those that cannot is flawed, as any NFSv4 operation may encounter a temporary condition and return NFS4ERR_DELAY at execution time. He suggested reclassifying operations based on their completion semantics, rather than specific opcodes, to allow for more flexibility in future updates.
review_comments.raw_body:
One concern I have with the "ops that can report delayed error"
formulation is that, in practice, any NFSv4 operation may encounter
a temporary condition and return NFS4ERR_DELAY at execution time.
Even a simple operation like GETFH may need to allocate reply
resources and fail transiently. As such, the ability to convey delay
is not a distinguishing property of a particular operation.

I think the relevant distinction is instead whether the client may
complete the syscall locally and defer authoritative execution
and/or result until a later point without violating its semantics.
Some operations fit that model, but others do not.

Even WRITE is not inherently in the "may be deferred" category;
that depends on the data path. For example, in a pNFS configuration
where WRITE is routed synchronously to an MDS, completion semantics
differ from a buffered writeback model. This suggests that the
classification should not be tied to specific opcodes, but to the
completion semantics of the operation in the context in which it
is executed.

Given that, it may be more useful for the spec to describe delegated
timestamp behavior in terms of these semantics:

   o Operations for which the client may lawfully acknowledge
   completion before authoritative server execution has completed
   may use delegated timestamp authority, subject to the constraints
   of that deferred-completion model.

   o Operations that require authoritative execution status before
   completion of the user-visible request must be executed synchronously
   and must not rely on deferred timestamp resolution.

This avoids per-op enumeration and allows future operations to be
classified based on their semantics rather than requiring explicit
specification updates.
---
Tom Haynes <loghyr@gmail.com>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author explained how delegated timestamps work and why they don't cause rolling back of ctime, stating that clamping updates to a range ensures correctness

Author clarified their intention behind adding a GETATTR to removexattr, stating it's for operations like unstable WRITEs and asynchronous COPY that can fail after server return
review_comments.raw_body:
We basically disable c/mtime updates on the delegation when it's handed
out and record the current time (A). We then clamp the delegated
timestamp update to between A and "now" when the SETATTR is done.
That's enough to ensure that nothing rolls backward but we can still
respect the client's update assuming the clocks are close enough.

---

Yeah, I phrased that poorly. I didn't mean something that would return
NFS4ERR_DELAY, but rather operations like unstable WRITEs and
asynchronous COPY operations (and anything else that falls into that
model) that can fail after the server has returned from the initial
operation.

review_comments.raw_body:
I thought the requirement to do a CB_GETATTR was if the GETATTR is
from a different client than the one holding the delegation, at least for
NFSv4.1/4.2? (Basically, if a 4.1/4.2 client holds a write delegation, it
is expected to "fake" the attributes and also reply with those faked attributes
to the server in a CB_GETATTR reply, so that other clients see the "faked"
attributes. Only after a DELEGRETURN does the server have the definitive
values for these attributes. At least that's the way I understood it?)

(Admittedly, for NFSv4.0, knowing of the client doing the GETATTR was
 the same one as the client holding the delegation wasn't often the case,
 but I basically ignore NFSv4.0 now. Imho NFSv4.0 delegations were
 so messy they weren't worth implementing.)

rick

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch set addresses issues related to delegated attributes in the NFS file system, specifically fixing problems with utimensat() and removexattr operations. The patches update inode timestamps and ctime after attribute changes, ensuring accurate and consistent behavior. The fixes are based on a previous version of the patch set, which has been updated with Fixes: tags for better tracking.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the proposed fix for xfstest generic/728 failing with delegated timestamps. The author acknowledged that the server's mtime and ctime are up-to-date after a REMOVEXATTR operation, making an additional SETATTR unnecessary. They explained their approach as relying on the fact that the server updates change_attr on operations that might not necessarily happen.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the patch's reliance on server behavior for delegated timestamps, specifically whether the server should update ctime/mtime on operations like REMOVEXATTR. The author agrees that there may be inconsistencies in server implementations and suggests that the patch relies too heavily on specific server behavior. They propose looking at how size is handled under delegation in RFC8881 as a model for timestamp handling.

> > > > > > stat() doesn't trigger a GETATTR because of the delegated timestamps, so

> > > > > generic/728.
> > > > > 
> > > > > diff --git a/fs/nfs/nfs42proc.c b/fs/nfs/nfs42proc.c
> > > > > index 7b3ca68fb4bb..ede1835a45b3 100644

> > > > > +                       nfs_update_delegated_mtime(inode);

> > > > >         return ret;
> > > > >  }
> > > > > 
> > > > 
> > > > What's the advantage of doing it this way?
> > > > 
> > > > You just sent a REMOVEXATTR operation to the server that will change
> > > > the mtime there. The server has the most up-to-date version of the
> > > > mtime and ctime at that point.
> > > 
> > > In presence of delegated attributes, Is the server required to update
> > > its mtime/ctime on an operation? As I mentioned, the linux server does
> > > not update its ctime/mtime for WRITE, CLONE, COPY.

> > > what the server supposed to do (and client rely on).
> > > 
> > 
> > (cc'ing Tom)
> > 
> > That is a very good point.
> > 
> > My interpretation was that delegated timestamps generally covered
> > writes, but SETATTR style operations that do anything beyond only
> > changing the mtime can't be cached.
> > 
> > We probably need some delstid spec clarification: for what operations
> > is the server required to disable timestamp updates when a write
> > delegation is outstanding?
> > 
> > In the case of nfsd, we disable timestamp updates for WRITE/COPY/CLONE
> > but not SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR.
> > 
> > How does the Hammerspace anvil behave? Does it disable c/mtime updates
> > for writes when there is an outstanding timestamp delegation like we're
> > doing in nfsd? If so, does it do the same for
> > SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR operations as well?
> 
> Jeff,
> 
> I think the right way to look at this is closer to how size is
> handled under delegation in RFC8881, rather than as a per-op rule.
> 
> In our implementation, because we are acting as an MDS and data I/O
> goes to DSes, we already treat size as effectively delegated when
> a write layout is outstanding. The MDS does not maintain authoritative
> size locally in that case. We may refresh size/timestamps internally
> (e.g., on GETATTR by querying DSes), but we don’t treat that as
> overriding the delegated authority.
> 
> For timestamps, our behavior is effectively the same model. When
> the client holds the relevant delegation, the server does not
> consider itself authoritative for ctime/mtime. If current values
> are needed, we can obtain them from the client (e.g., via CB_GETATTR),
> and the client must present the delegation stateid to demonstrate
> that authority. So the authority follows the delegation, not the
> specific operation.
> 
> That said, I don’t think we’ve fully resolved the semantics for all
> metadata-style ops either. WRITE and SETATTR are clear in our model,
> but for things like CLONE/COPY/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR, we’ve likely
> been relying on assumptions rather than a fully consistent rule.
> I.e., CLONE and COPY we just pass through to the DS and we don't
> implement SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR.
> 
> So the spec question, as I see it, is not whether REMOVEXATTR (or
> any particular op) should update ctime/mtime, but whether delegated
> timestamps are meant to follow the same attribute-authority model
> as delegated size in RFC8881. If so, then we expect that the server
> should query the client via CB_GETATTR to return updated ctime/mtime
> after such operations while the delegation is outstanding.
> 

The dilemma we have is: because we _do_ allow local processes to stat()
files that have an outstanding write delegation, we can never allow the
ctime in particular to roll backward (modulo clock jumps).

If we're dealing with changes that have been cached in the client and
are being lazily flushed out, then we can't update the timestamp when
that operation occurs. The time of the RPC to flush the changes will
almost certainly be later than the cached timestamps on the client that
will eventually be set, so when the client comes back we'd end up
violating the rollback rule.

Our only option is to freeze timestamp updates on anything that might
represent such an operation. So far, we only do that on WRITE and COPY
operations -- in general, operations that require an open file, since
FMODE_NOCMTIME is attached to the file.

Some SETATTRs that only update the mtime and atime can be cached on the
client by virtue of the fact that it's authoritative for timestamps.
There are some exceptions though:

- atime-only updates can't be cached since the ctime won't change with
a timestamp update if the mtime didn't change

- if you set the mtime to a time that is later than the time you got
the delegation from the server, but earlier than the current time, you
can't cache that. The ctime would be later than the mtime in that case,
and we don't have a mechanism to handle that in a delegated timestamp
SETATTR.

I don't see how you could reasonably buffer a SETXATTR or REMOVEXATTR
operation to be sent later. These need to be done synchronously since
they could always fail for some reason and we don't have a mechanism at
the syscall layer to handle a deferred error. They also only update the
ctime and not the mtime, and we have no mechanism to do that with
delegated timestamps.

Based on that, I think the client and server both need to ignore the
timestamp delegation on a SETXATTR or REMOVEXATTR. The server should
update the ctime and the client needs to send a trailing GETATTR on the
REMOVEXATTR compound in order to get it and the change attr.

Exactly what this patch does, fwiw...
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

---

> operations, then every new operation added to the protocol creates
> a fresh ambiguity until the spec is updated again. It seems better

> the operation is synchronously visible, can be deferred/cached at
> the client, and whether it affects only ctime versus mtime/atime -
> so future operations can be classified without reopening the base
> rule.
>

I think we probably need some guidance in the spec, and I think that
guidance comes down to: operations that don't have a way to report a
delayed error condition can't be buffered on the client and must
continue to be done synchronously even if a delegation is held.

By way of example: if I do a write() on the client I can buffer that
because userland can eventually do an fsync() to see if it succeeded.
This is not true for syscalls like setxattr() or removexattr(), or most
syscalls that result in a SETATTR operation (chmod(), chown(), etc).

They must be done synchronously because:

1/ there's no way to update only the ctime in a delegated timestamp
update

...and...

2/ these syscalls can fail, so we can't return from them until we know
the outcome.

How best to phrase this guidance, I'm not sure...

The reviewer pointed out that the patch relies on the server updating its mtime/ctime after receiving a REMOVEXATTR operation, but noted that this might not be true for all implementations (e.g., Hammerspace anvil). The author agrees with the reviewer's point and suggests that there is a need for clarification in the specification regarding what operations require disabling timestamp updates when a write delegation is outstanding. Specifically, they mention that nfsd disables timestamp updates for WRITE/COPY/CLONE but not SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR.

> > > > > > > > >         trace_nfs4_removexattr(inode, name, ret);

> > > > > > > > 
> > > > > > > > What's the advantage of doing it this way?
> > > > > > > > 
> > > > > > > > You just sent a REMOVEXATTR operation to the server that will change
> > > > > > > > the mtime there. The server has the most up-to-date version of the
> > > > > > > > mtime and ctime at that point.

> > > > > > > In presence of delegated attributes, Is the server required to update
> > > > > > > its mtime/ctime on an operation? As I mentioned, the linux server does
> > > > > > > not update its ctime/mtime for WRITE, CLONE, COPY.
> > > > > > > 
> > > > > > > Is possible that
> > > > > > > some implementations might be different and also do not update the
> > > > > > > ctime/mtime on REMOVEXATTR?
> > > > > > > 
> > > > > > > Therefore I was suggesting that the patch
> > > > > > > relies on the fact that it would receive an updated value. Of course
> > > > > > > perhaps all implementations are done the same as the linux server and
> > > > > > > my point is moot. I didn't see anything in the spec that clarifies
> > > > > > > what the server supposed to do (and client rely on).
> > > > > > > 
> > > > > > 
> > > > > > (cc'ing Tom)
> > > > > > 
> > > > > > That is a very good point.
> > > > > > 
> > > > > > My interpretation was that delegated timestamps generally covered
> > > > > > writes, but SETATTR style operations that do anything beyond only
> > > > > > changing the mtime can't be cached.
> > > > > > 
> > > > > > We probably need some delstid spec clarification: for what operations
> > > > > > is the server required to disable timestamp updates when a write
> > > > > > delegation is outstanding?
> > > > > > 
> > > > > > In the case of nfsd, we disable timestamp updates for WRITE/COPY/CLONE
> > > > > > but not SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR.
> > > > > > 
> > > > > > How does the Hammerspace anvil behave? Does it disable c/mtime updates
> > > > > > for writes when there is an outstanding timestamp delegation like we're
> > > > > > doing in nfsd? If so, does it do the same for
> > > > > > SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR operations as well?
> > > > > 
> > > > > Jeff,
> > > > > 
> > > > > I think the right way to look at this is closer to how size is
> > > > > handled under delegation in RFC8881, rather than as a per-op rule.
> > > > > 
> > > > > In our implementation, because we are acting as an MDS and data I/O
> > > > > goes to DSes, we already treat size as effectively delegated when
> > > > > a write layout is outstanding. The MDS does not maintain authoritative
> > > > > size locally in that case. We may refresh size/timestamps internally
> > > > > (e.g., on GETATTR by querying DSes), but we don’t treat that as
> > > > > overriding the delegated authority.
> > > > > 
> > > > > For timestamps, our behavior is effectively the same model. When
> > > > > the client holds the relevant delegation, the server does not
> > > > > consider itself authoritative for ctime/mtime. If current values
> > > > > are needed, we can obtain them from the client (e.g., via CB_GETATTR),
> > > > > and the client must present the delegation stateid to demonstrate
> > > > > that authority. So the authority follows the delegation, not the
> > > > > specific operation.
> > > > > 
> > > > > That said, I don’t think we’ve fully resolved the semantics for all
> > > > > metadata-style ops either. WRITE and SETATTR are clear in our model,
> > > > > but for things like CLONE/COPY/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR, we’ve likely
> > > > > been relying on assumptions rather than a fully consistent rule.
> > > > > I.e., CLONE and COPY we just pass through to the DS and we don't
> > > > > implement SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR.
> > > > > 
> > > > > So the spec question, as I see it, is not whether REMOVEXATTR (or
> > > > > any particular op) should update ctime/mtime, but whether delegated
> > > > > timestamps are meant to follow the same attribute-authority model
> > > > > as delegated size in RFC8881. If so, then we expect that the server
> > > > > should query the client via CB_GETATTR to return updated ctime/mtime
> > > > > after such operations while the delegation is outstanding.
> > > > > 
> > > > 
> > > > The dilemma we have is: because we _do_ allow local processes to stat()
> > > > files that have an outstanding write delegation, we can never allow the
> > > > ctime in particular to roll backward (modulo clock jumps).
> > > 
> > > I agree we do not want visible ctime rollback, but I do not see how
> > > that can be guaranteed from delegated timestamps alone when the
> > > authoritative timestamp may be generated on a different node with
> > > a different clock and the object may change during the CB_GETATTR
> > > window. That seems to require either monotonic clamping of exposed
> > > ctime, or treating change_attr rather than ctime as the real
> > > serialization signal.
> > > 

We basically disable c/mtime updates on the delegation when it's handed
out and record the current time (A). We then clamp the delegated
timestamp update to between A and "now" when the SETATTR is done.
That's enough to ensure that nothing rolls backward but we can still
respect the client's update assuming the clocks are close enough.

> > > > 
> > > > If we're dealing with changes that have been cached in the client and
> > > > are being lazily flushed out, then we can't update the timestamp when
> > > > that operation occurs. The time of the RPC to flush the changes will
> > > > almost certainly be later than the cached timestamps on the client that
> > > > will eventually be set, so when the client comes back we'd end up
> > > > violating the rollback rule.
> > > > 
> > > > Our only option is to freeze timestamp updates on anything that might
> > > > represent such an operation. So far, we only do that on WRITE and COPY
> > > > operations -- in general, operations that require an open file, since
> > > > FMODE_NOCMTIME is attached to the file.
> > > > 
> > > > Some SETATTRs that only update the mtime and atime can be cached on the
> > > > client by virtue of the fact that it's authoritative for timestamps.
> > > > There are some exceptions though:
> > > > 
> > > > - atime-only updates can't be cached since the ctime won't change with
> > > > a timestamp update if the mtime didn't change
> > > > 
> > > > - if you set the mtime to a time that is later than the time you got
> > > > the delegation from the server, but earlier than the current time, you
> > > > can't cache that. The ctime would be later than the mtime in that case,
> > > > and we don't have a mechanism to handle that in a delegated timestamp
> > > > SETATTR.
> > > > 
> > > > I don't see how you could reasonably buffer a SETXATTR or REMOVEXATTR
> > > > operation to be sent later. These need to be done synchronously since
> > > > they could always fail for some reason and we don't have a mechanism at
> > > > the syscall layer to handle a deferred error. They also only update the
> > > > ctime and not the mtime, and we have no mechanism to do that with
> > > > delegated timestamps.
> > > > 
> > > > Based on that, I think the client and server both need to ignore the
> > > > timestamp delegation on a SETXATTR or REMOVEXATTR. The server should
> > > > update the ctime and the client needs to send a trailing GETATTR on the
> > > > REMOVEXATTR compound in order to get it and the change attr.
> > > 
> > > One concern I have with a per-op formulation is extensibility. If
> > > delegated timestamp behavior is defined by enumerating specific
> > > operations, then every new operation added to the protocol creates
> > > a fresh ambiguity until the spec is updated again. It seems better
> > > to define the behavior in terms of operation properties - e.g., whether
> > > the operation is synchronously visible, can be deferred/cached at
> > > the client, and whether it affects only ctime versus mtime/atime -
> > > so future operations can be classified without reopening the base
> > > rule.
> > > 
> > > I.e., I can't tell if you want me to update the spec with
> > > guidance per-op or you are just documenting what you did.
> > > 
> > > 
> > 
> > I think we probably need some guidance in the spec, and I think that
> > guidance comes down to: operations that don't have a way to report a
> > delayed error condition can't be buffered on the client and must
> > continue to be done synchronously even if a delegation is held.
> > 
> > By way of example: if I do a write() on the client I can buffer that
> > because userland can eventually do an fsync() to see if it succeeded.
> > This is not true for syscalls like setxattr() or removexattr(), or most
> > syscalls that result in a SETATTR operation (chmod(), chown(), etc).
> > 
> > They must be done synchronously because:
> > 
> > 1/ there's no way to update only the ctime in a delegated timestamp
> > update
> > 
> > ...and...
> > 
> > 2/ these syscalls can fail, so we can't return from them until we know
> > the outcome.
> > 
> > How best to phrase this guidance, I'm not sure...
> > -- 
> > Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> 
> One concern I have with the "ops that can report delayed error"
> formulation is that, in practice, any NFSv4 operation may encounter
> a temporary condition and return NFS4ERR_DELAY at execution time.
> Even a simple operation like GETFH may need to allocate reply
> resources and fail transiently. As such, the ability to convey delay
> is not a distinguishing property of a particular operation.
> 
> I think the relevant distinction is instead whether the client may
> complete the syscall locally and defer authoritative execution
> and/or result until a later point without violating its semantics.
> Some operations fit that model, but others do not.
> 
> Even WRITE is not inherently in the "may be deferred" category;
> that depends on the data path. For example, in a pNFS configuration
> where WRITE is routed synchronously to an MDS, completion semantics
> differ from a buffered writeback model. This suggests that the
> classification should not be tied to specific opcodes, but to the
> completion semantics of the operation in the context in which it
> is executed.
> 

Yeah, I phrased that poorly. I didn't mean something that would return
NFS4ERR_DELAY, but rather operations like unstable WRITEs and
asynchronous COPY operations (and anything else that falls into that
model) that can fail after the server has returned from the initial
operation.

> Given that, it may be more useful for the spec to describe delegated
> timestamp behavior in terms of these semantics:
> 
>    o Operations for which the client may lawfully acknowledge
>    completion before authoritative server execution has completed
>    may use delegated timestamp authority, subject to the constraints
>    of that deferred-completion model.
> 
>    o Operations that require authoritative execution status before
>    completion of the user-visible request must be executed synchronously
>    and must not rely on deferred timestamp resolution.
> 
> This avoids per-op enumeration and allows future operations to be
> classified based on their semantics rather than requiring explicit
> specification updates.

Ack. Sounds good to me.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Olga Kornievskaia
review_comments.summary: Olga Kornievskaia proposed an alternative fix for the generic/728 test failure, noting that the original approach relies on server behavior that may not always occur (e.g., updating change_attribute on writes or clone operations). She suggested modifying the nfs42_proc_removexattr function to update the delegated mtime and invalidate the inode's cache when dealing with delegated attributes.

review_comments.author: Olga Kornievskaia
review_comments.summary: Olga Kornievskaia pointed out that the patch relies on an assumption about server behavior in delegated attribute scenarios, specifically that the server updates ctime/mtime for REMOVEXATTR operations. However, she noted that some implementations (e.g., Hammerspace anvil) may not update ctime/mtime for these operations, and requested clarification of the NFSv4.2 specification regarding timestamp updates under delegation.

review_comments.author: Olga Kornievskaia
review_comments.summary: Olga Kornievskaia pointed out that the patch relies on a server behavior assumption, specifically that it updates mtime/ctime on REMOVEXATTR operations in presence of delegated attributes. She noted that the Linux server does not update ctime/mtime for WRITE, CLONE, and COPY operations, and questioned whether other implementations might behave similarly. She suggested clarifying the delegation timestamp specification to determine when servers should disable timestamp updates during write delegations.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 27, 2026 at 4:02 PM Thomas Haynes <loghyr@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Fri, Mar 27, 2026 at 03:36:12PM -0800, Olga Kornievskaia wrote:
> > On Fri, Mar 27, 2026 at 2:22 PM Thomas Haynes <loghyr@gmail.com> wrote:
> > >
> > > On Fri, Mar 27, 2026 at 12:59:54PM -0800, Jeff Layton wrote:

> > > > > > > >
> > > > > > > > xfstest generic/728 fails with delegated timestamps. The client does a
> > > > > > > > removexattr and then a stat to test the ctime, which doesn't change. The
> > > > > > > > stat() doesn't trigger a GETATTR because of the delegated timestamps, so
> > > > > > > > it relies on the cached ctime, which is wrong.
> > > > > > > >
> > > > > > > > The setxattr compound has a trailing GETATTR, which ensures that its
> > > > > > > > ctime gets updated. Follow the same strategy with removexattr.
> > > > > > >
> > > > > > > This approach relies on the fact that the server the serves delegated
> > > > > > > attributes would update change_attr on operations which might now
> > > > > > > necessarily happen (ie, linux server does not update change_attribute
> > > > > > > on writes or clone). I propose an alternative fix for the failing
> > > > > > > generic/728.

> > > > > > > diff --git a/fs/nfs/nfs42proc.c b/fs/nfs/nfs42proc.c

> > > > > > >         return ret;
> > > > > > >  }
> > > > > > >
> > > > > >
> > > > > > What's the advantage of doing it this way?
> > > > > >
> > > > > > You just sent a REMOVEXATTR operation to the server that will change
> > > > > > the mtime there. The server has the most up-to-date version of the
> > > > > > mtime and ctime at that point.
> > > > >
> > > > > In presence of delegated attributes, Is the server required to update
> > > > > its mtime/ctime on an operation? As I mentioned, the linux server does
> > > > > not update its ctime/mtime for WRITE, CLONE, COPY.

> > > > > Is possible that

> > > > > Therefore I was suggesting that the patch

> > > > > my point is moot. I didn't see anything in the spec that clarifies

> > > > >
> > > >
> > > > (cc'ing Tom)
> > > >
> > > > That is a very good point.
> > > >
> > > > My interpretation was that delegated timestamps generally covered
> > > > writes, but SETATTR style operations that do anything beyond only
> > > > changing the mtime can't be cached.
> > > >
> > > > We probably need some delstid spec clarification: for what operations
> > > > is the server required to disable timestamp updates when a write
> > > > delegation is outstanding?
> > > >
> > > > In the case of nfsd, we disable timestamp updates for WRITE/COPY/CLONE
> > > > but not SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR.
> > > >
> > > > How does the Hammerspace anvil behave? Does it disable c/mtime updates
> > > > for writes when there is an outstanding timestamp delegation like we're
> > > > doing in nfsd? If so, does it do the same for
> > > > SETATTR/SETXATTR/REMOVEXATTR operations as well?
> > >
> > > Jeff,
> > >
> > > I think the right way to look at this is closer to how size is
> > > handled under delegation in RFC8881, rather than as a per-op rule.
> > >
> > > In our implementation, because we are acting as an MDS and data I/O
> > > goes to DSes, we already treat size as effectively delegated when
> > > a write layout is outstanding. The MDS does not maintain authoritative
> > > size locally in that case. We may refresh size/timestamps internally
> > > (e.g., on GETATTR by querying DSes), but we don’t treat that as
> > > overriding the delegated authority.
> > >
> > > For timestamps, our behavior is effectively the same model. When
> > > the client holds the relevant delegation, the server does not
> > > consider itself authoritative for ctime/mtime. If current values
> > > are needed, we can obtain them from the client (e.g., via CB_GETATTR),
> > > and the client must present the delegation stateid to demonstrate

> > > specific operation.
> >
> > What happens when the client holding the attribute delegation (it's
> > the authority) is doing the query? Is it the server's responsibility
